content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 22, 1990

Jeannine English
Executive Director
Little Hoover Commission
1303 J Street, Suite 270
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-378

Dear Ms. English:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning
your duties as executive director of the Little Hoover Commission
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act ("the Act").1/ This letter confirms the telephone advice
provided to you on Wednesday, June 20, 1990.

We do not provide advice concerning provisions of law other
than the Act. You may wish to consult with the Attorney General's
Office regarding other provisions of law such as Section 1090
which prohibits government officials from having an interest in
contracts with their agencies.

QUESTION

Your spouse is under annual contract with the Little Hoover
Commission, the governmental agency for which you serve as
executive director. His contract is up for renewal. What, if
anything, should you do with respect to the pending contract
renewal process?

CONCLUSION

The renewal of your spouse's contract is a governmental
decision which will affect your economic interests. Therefore,
you may not participate in the renewal of the contract or use your
official position to influence this governmental decision.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-90-378
Page 2


FACTS

The Little Hoover Commission (the "Commission") has retained
private legal counsel, Howard L. Dickstein, to serve as its
general counsel. The Commission has entered into annual contracts
with Mr. Dickstein's law firm, Dickstein & Merin, for this purpose
since 1986. Sometime after the law firm's last contract was
renewed by the Commission, you and Mr. Dickstein married.

According to the information provided in your letter, the
Commission itself selects the general counsel and approves his
fixed price contracts on an annual basis, a process in which you
do not participate. In our telephone conversation of June 15,
1990 you provided the following additional information on this
subject. The Commission considers the terms of the contract with
Dickstein & Merin, a partnership, in private session. The
executive director has no direct authority to into into a
contractual agreement on behalf of the Commission for the purpose
of selecting or retaining its private legal counsel.

The only activity carried out by your staff in connection
with the contract is to furnish the Commission with terms of the
previous contracts relating to the duties and scope of the
responsibilities of the legal counsel and the amount of
remuneration paid for his services. Consideration of the renewal
of legal counsel is placed automatically on the Commission's
agenda in the month of June of each calendar year. Support staff
types the new contract.


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 sets forth the general rule concerning
potential conflicts of interest confronting public officials. It
states that no public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt
to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or
she has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his immediate family or
on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more.

* * *

File No. A-90-378
Page 3


(c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating $250 or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

* * *

For purposes of Section 87103, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103 (a) and (c).

As executive director of the Little Hoover Commission, you
are a public official.  (Section 82048; Regulation 18700(a), copy
enclosed.)  Your spouse has an interest in a law partnership,
Dickstein & Merin, which is presumably worth more than $1,000.
Under Section 87103 you have an indirect interest in your spouse's
law firm.  Accordingly, you must disqualify yourself from
participating in any decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the law partnership which
is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

In the situation you describe, your spouse is directly
involved in the decision before the Commission which will have
some financial effect on his law partnership.  Under our
regulations, such financial effect is deemed to be material.
(See Regulation 18702.1(a)(2), copy enclosed.)

In your letter to the Commission and in our subsequent
telephone conversation, you stated that you do not participate in
any manner in the contract renewal process.2/  The only activities
performed by your staff in connection with the contract in
question appear to be clerical in nature.  Making or participating
in the making of a governmental decision does not include actions
of public officials which are solely ministerial, secretarial,

2/     You may not participate in any governmental decision, not just
the contract renewal process, that may have a material financial
effect on any economic interest specified in Section 87103,
including, but not limited to, the law partnership.  See our
brochure entitled California's Conflict of Interest Law for Public
Officials, copy enclosed.

File No. A-90-378
Page 4


manual or clerical in nature.  (Regulation 18700.1(d); Cano Advice
Letter No. I-90-190; Flandrick Advice Letter No. A-86-146, copies
enclosed).

     However, please note that "participate" in the making of a
governmental decision is interpreted very broadly by the Fair
Political Practices Commission.  Regulation 18700, subsection (b)
provides that a public official "makes a governmental decision"
when he or she (1) votes on a matter; (2) appoints a person; (3)
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action;
or (4) enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or
her agency.  Subsection (c) of Regulation 18700(c) provides that a
public official "participates in the making of a governmental
decision" when he or she (1) negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person
regarding the decision; or (2) advises or makes recommendations to
the decision-maker, either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review.  (Regulation 18700; Combs Advice
Letter No. A-89-177; McHugh Advice Letter No. I-89-443, copies
enclosed.)

     This means that you may not take any action to approve,
conditionally approve, or disapprove, the renewal of the contract
or to advise or make recommendations concerning any term or
provision of the renewal contract.

     Furthermore, please note that "using official position to
influence" a governmental decision also is interpreted very
broadly by the Fair Political Practices Commission, in Regulation
18700.1(a), as follows:

          With regard to a governmental decision which
     is within or before an official's agency or an
     agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
     control of his or her agency, the official is
     attempting to use his or her official position to
     influence the decision if, for the purpose of
     influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
     appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
     any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
     agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not
     limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
     on behalf of a business entity, client, or
     customer.

          Regulation 18700.1(a) copy enclosed.
     See McHugh Advice Letter, supra.

     This means that you may not communicate with any of the
members of the Commission or your staff with respect to the
contract with Dickstein & Merin.

File No. A-90-378
Page 5


     I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any additional questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [illegible]
                                        By:  Deanne Stone
                                             Counsel, Legal Division
